question: How to design a reference plane switching area to prevent the reflow path from breaking when high-speed differential pairs traverse multi-layer structures?
options: A
A multi-layer superimposed microstrip line structure is used to align the reference planes up and down to construct a continuous pathway
B
Ground via bridging is introduced at the reference plane switching to ensure that the return current is continuous and the length of the impedance jump zone is matched
C
Compensate for the copper application area below the differential pair path, and set up additional protective rings to enhance the anti-interference ability
D
Use a discontinuous reference plane design and add a metallized window at the jumping position to reduce the risk of capacitive coupling interference
E
None of the above options are correct

question: When an AC-coupled capacitor needs to be connected to the differential pair end, how to ensure the internal impedance continuity?
options: A
The coupling capacitor and the trace position do not need to be strictly aligned, but only pay attention to the capacitance matching and the standard
B
Use a symmetrical layout and equal capacitance values while matching the trace length near the coupling capacitor, and arrange as close to the load or receiver as possible to reduce reflections
C
Any capacitance can be used, as long as the overall capacitance meets the bandwidth of the system, without considering the specific wiring form too much
D
Additional ground planes are added before and after the coupling point to maintain general electrical stability to accommodate reflected changes caused by different load changes
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What are the two types of critical SI problems that differential pairs can introduce into turns? How to alleviate at the same time?
options: A

Changes in the distance between the differential lines after turning will cause electrical lengths to vary and require the introduction of additional delay compensation circuits for physical compensation
B
Latency mismatch and pattern transition issues are superimposed and can be corrected asymmetry by adding bypass paths and end networks
C
Impedance discontinuity and asymmetric delay, the impedance at the bend can be compensated by the equivalent line width, and the delay consistency can be maintained by symmetrical turning
D
Multiple turns will cause high-frequency interference superposition, and the risk of crosstalk can be reduced by covering the differential path in a large area of copper coating
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How can crosstalk be controlled to meet the SI budget of systems above 50 Gbps when multiple sets of high-speed differential pairs are routed in parallel?
options: A
When designing differential pairing, only focus on line length consistency rather than spacing control, which can effectively ensure that the mode is not converted and the delay is consistent
B

Sufficient spacing between differential pairs (>4H) is adopted, with continuous and uniform coupling of reference layers, and ground wire isolation or serpentine staggered wiring method is used if necessary
C
Crosstalk between differential pairs can be controlled mainly by terminal matching, and there is no need to consider the routing spacing and the continuity of the reference layer
D

All differential correspondence should pass through the blind hole layer structure of the same structure at the same time to ensure phase consistency, and at the same time, no simulation can save wiring complexity
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How can high-speed differential pairs model vias to improve reflection control accuracy in blind and buried via HDI structures?
options: A

When modeling, only the length of the center column of the via needs to be considered to match the signal trace, and other structures can be simplified to an equivalent impedance network model to speed up the simulation
B
Model vias using 3D full-wave simulation, including pad, reverse pad, and ground via structures, adjust stub lengths or add backdrilling to control impedance changes caused by vias
C
In high-frequency design, HDI vias have little effect on reflection, and their behavior can be simply estimated by using a centralized parameter model as the design basis
D

To simplify the modeling process of multilayer HDI boards, differential pair vias can be unified into a standardized Via template regardless of actual layout offsets and interconnect structure details
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What are the main mechanisms for common-mode noise in differential signals? How to minimize in wiring?
options: A

Common-mode noise is often caused by high-frequency interference on the power side, so common-mode effects can be eliminated by adding a separate filter to each differential line and adding a shield to the periphery of the PCB
B

Common mode noise comes from inconsistent wire length, coupling asymmetry and discontinuous reference surface, and the wiring should be matched and symmetrically arranged to ensure the complete return path
C

The common-mode noise on the differential pair is mainly due to the impedance discontinuity caused by the test probe, so it is necessary to add a compensation network and additional terminal suppression reflection near the probe
D
As long as equal differential pair routing is used, all common-mode noise issues are naturally offset without additional design optimization of reference strata or shielding structures
E
None of the above options are correct

question: In PCB interlayer stackup design, is it reasonable to place differential pairs on different layers above and below? Please give possible consequences and improvement strategies.
options: A

The upper and lower layer wiring will lead to coupling failure and unequal delay, which will seriously affect the differentiality. Parallel wiring on the same floor or introducing redundant compensation lines in the same direction on the inner layer should improve symmetry
B

The differential pairs distributed in different layers can better isolate electromagnetic interference, and the equivalent coupling model can be reconstructed by adjusting the thickness and metal density to alleviate the coupling problem
C

If the differential pairs are on adjacent layers and mirror symmetry is maintained, the coupling characteristics can be fully reproduced and any SI problems can be avoided, so this strategy is recommended for routing optimization when space is limited
D

As long as the number of vias is the same, the differential line can maintain consistency even if it is distributed across different layers, and all possible inconsistencies can be calibrated by adding an ESD protection circuit at the end
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why is GSSG construction recommended over SGSG or SSSS for differential pair cabling?
options: A
SGSG structure provides more flexible power wiring solutions, while GSSG structure requires too high RF performance and is not suitable for general-purpose high-speed differential pairs
B

The GSSG structure provides symmetric electromagnetic field coupling and lower common-mode radiation, reducing crosstalk and easier controlling impedance, while SGSG and SSSS have insufficient symmetry or ground reference discontinuity
C
The SSSS structure can automatically match all impedance paths in multi-layer wiring, so its performance is far better than that of GSSG, making it the preferred solution in high-frequency boards
D
Both SGSG and SSSS structures have stronger anti-interference ability under high-speed signals, while GSSG has continuity problems in the reflow path
E
None of the above options are correct

question: When a serpentine compensation structure occurs in equimetric routing, how can its effect on crosstalk and insertion loss be controlled?
options: A

Keep the serpentine line spacing greater than 3 times the line width, reduce the coupling length, control the number of segments should not be too much, and avoid using it in the high-speed critical path
B
Appropriately increase the number of serpentine segments and staggered arrangement in areas with high wiring density, and increase the vias between segments to stabilize the signal phase relationship
C

Serpentine compensation wiring is preferentially used on the low-speed control path, and the high-speed path solves the problem of isometric deviation by adding compensation devices at the receiving end
D

Crosstalk effect can be reduced by increasing the interconnection ground wire between serpentine segments, and the alternate serpentine design of different layers can be used to avoid high-frequency distortion during wiring
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What kind of SI problem occurs when there is a via stub at one end of the differential pair but a backdrill at the other end? How to deal with it?
options: A

It will cause symmetry failure to cause common-mode noise and reflection, and the symmetrical electrical characteristics should be achieved by using backdrill at both ends or adjusting the length of the stub through simulation
B
Reflection problems of stubs can be eliminated as long as one of the ends is used with backdrill, symmetry has no real effect in differential pairs
C
If the differential pair length matching accuracy is within ±50 mil, even the presence of stubs will not have a significant impact on high-speed transmission and no additional processing is required
D
Stub effect can be compensated by adding an impedance-matched resistor to the edge of the PCB, and common-mode noise can be controlled without the need for a backdrill structure
E
None of the above options are correct

question: In a differential pair with strict requirements for length matching, if there are heterogeneous traces but the total length matches, can they still be considered equal in length? Why?
options: A

No, just looking at the geometric length ignores the difference between the dielectric constant and the propagation delay of different layers. Skew matching should be considered and compensated by the time delay
B

It can be regarded as equal length, because the high-speed differential signal pays more attention to the consistency of the physical wiring path, and the difference in dielectric constant can be uniformly handled by later calibration
C
If the differential pair matches exactly in terms of physical length, the signal transmission time error is negligible, and the actual transmission performance will not degrade significantly
D

At the industrial-grade signal rate, the propagation difference caused by the differentiated trace is much smaller than the jitter caused by EMI interference, which can be ignored by the shielding layer compensation
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What are the two types of critical distortions that occur when a high-speed differential pair passes through a plane gap or a reference layer breakpoint? How to route circumvention?
options: A
It will lead to differential impedance mutations and delay mismatches, so bypass matching capacitors and current limiting resistors should be used to reduce sudden peak interference
B

Causes common-mode noise and disruption of the return path. The reference layer should be bridged through the ground via the ground via network to avoid the high-speed differential line in the gap or power supply reference area
C
It is easy to cause heat-induced distortion and frequency microdrift, so it is recommended to add a metal reinforcement layer to stabilize the thermal field distribution in the breakpoint area
D
Reference layer cutting triggers a crosstalk enhancement mechanism that suppresses the noise propagation path through an embedded signal buffer module and a decoupled network
E
None of the above options are correct

question: In a multi-master broadcast structure where multiple sources drive the same differential pair load, what SI consistency requirements must be met in the cabling design?
options: A

All main driving paths should be of equal length and continuous impedance. The branch point is arranged close to the receiving end, and the source is matched in series with resistance to isolate the reflection source
B
The multi-main cabling structure must adopt high and low cross-scheduling methods, and introduce termination adapters to prevent two-way transmission path conflicts and jitter accumulation
C
Adopt a symmetrical tree branch topology and insert an active retimer to ensure balanced latency and level integrity over the entire path range
D

The pseudo-differential backup channel is used in the multi-master structure and combined with CRC verification to effectively avoid the failure of the main signal path affecting the stability of the synchronization mechanism
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Is it still necessary to maintain a symmetrical layout in very short differential line applications (<200mil)? Why?
options: A
It can be regarded as single-ended signal processing, with only a focus on DC balance and no need to maintain symmetry
B
Asymmetry does not cause serious effects, and potential common-mode interference can be suppressed by a post-stage filter
C
Symmetry is still required, even if the line length is short, asymmetry will produce common-mode components, affecting the reception accuracy and increasing the radiation risk
D
As long as the final length of the two wires matches, the path bend and reference formation can be flexibly handled to simplify the routing difficulty
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What routing strategies should be used for differential pairs to traverse multiple large capacitor pad areas?
options: A
Avoid wiring close to the edge of the pad, try to bypass and keep the reference surface continuous, and adjust the shape of the pad if necessary to avoid sudden impedance changes
B
The pad center traversal strategy is adopted, and the parasitic effect and reflection interference in the pad area are compensated by widening the line width
C
Partially overlapping pads can be allowed, and reflection problems can be mitigated by simply using backdrill to control the stub effect in the high frequency band
D
Go straight along the shortest path of pad clearance, sacrifice a certain symmetry in exchange for wiring space efficiency, and compensate for the inconsistency with software in the later stage
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to evaluate the phase noise introduced by high-speed differential pairs of serpentine traces? How to control in cabling?
options: A
TDR is mainly used to analyze the line impedance, and the serpentine structure itself does not introduce phase changes, and its influence can be ignored
B

Use full-wave simulation to evaluate the proximal crosstalk and phase jitter, and the serpentine segment should be spaced more than 3W and the cumulative length of the coupling zone should be controlled by staggered strategy
C
The differential serpentine segment only needs to be of equal length, and the lack of symmetry or close bypass will not significantly affect the clock phase stability
D
The crosstalk path is analyzed by SPICE model, and the terminal resistance is added to the critical region to reduce the effect of delay inconsistency on the phase
E
None of the above options are correct

question: When a differential pair traverses a high-power multiphase Buck power supply network, what kind of noise coupling should be most wary of? How to isolate?
options: A
Differential mode noise will cause asymmetric distortion of the clock signal, and ferrite should be shielded in series with ferrite in the power supply loop to shield the decoupling path
B

The change of high-frequency magnetic flux will cause synchronous oscillation in the inductive circuit, and the voltage control terminal filtering needs to be applied to stabilize the load switching noise
C
The staggered switching frequency will cause spectral aliasing, and the differential line should be noise reduced by a π-type compensation structure near the Buck control IC
D

Be wary of the common mode noise of dv/dt coupling, and the differential line should be close to the complete reference layer in the inner layer, and the ground protection should be added and the distance between the Buck switch node and at least 3W should be maintained
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What are the mechanical and electrical constraints that need to be met when using backdrill technology to remove via stubs?
options: A

Mechanically, the minimum residual stub should be retained (generally > 6mil to avoid puncture), and the target bandwidth should be matched electrically to control reflection and verify the stub suppression effect through simulation
B
Back drilling is mainly used for low-speed signals, and its mechanical requirements are not high, only the drilling position needs to be controlled to align with the pad
C
The stub can be completely removed within the allowable range of mechanical tolerance, and the stub is no longer involved in the electrical behavior simulation and can be ignored
D
Back drilling can be done as long as a material with high tensile strength is used, regardless of the effect of stub length on the reflection curve
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why is it recommended to add ground via rings in differential pair via design? How to lay it out?
options: A
Construct an equivalent compensation network through ground vias and power layers to improve the stability and robustness of the interconnection
B
Ground via is used to adjust the impedance continuity and via parasitic effect, and the impedance inverse compensation structure is constructed in the stacking of multi-layer plates
C
The ground via ring provides a continuous reflow path to reduce common-mode radiation, and the signal via should be equidistantly symmetrically enclosed to form a closed reflow channel
D
There is no need for ground vias around the signal vias, and only a series of resistance needs to be added to the beginning of the differential pair to maintain balance
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What impedance and mode conversion issues should be paid attention to when converting differential wires to single-ended signals (e.g., DC detection)?
options: A
The differential signal can be directly switched to single-ended use without additional compensation network, just keep the impedance of the detection point consistent with the source
B

A balanced matching network should be introduced at the conversion point to prevent the reflection caused by differential mode to common mode, and a matching resistor should be added to the ground end of the detection point to ensure impedance consistency
C
It is recommended to add a low-pass filter to the conversion section to eliminate the high-frequency harmonics caused by mode mismatch while keeping the source impedance unchanged
D
Ground any side of the differential line, lead the signal line out on the other side for sampling, and then use the back-end DSP to compensate for common mode to avoid the mode conversion problem
E
None of the above options are correct

question: If the high-speed differential pair has three upper and lower layer jumps in a certain section of wiring, how to evaluate the cumulative effect of crosstalk? How should it be designed to circumvent it?
options: A

The return interruption caused by the skipping layer can be verified by simulation, and the jumping position needs to be bridged with ground via holes, and it is recommended that the number of skipping layers should be controlled within two times
B

Multi-stage ground vias should be used to surround and restore the structure with a full reference plane to reduce the phase interference reflection caused by cross-layer coupling and asymmetric paths
C

After layer skipping, the electrical compensation network and symmetric wiring strategy can be used with delay equalization to enhance the anti-crosstalk ability and alleviate the problem of impedance jump mismatch
D

It is proposed to introduce three-dimensional interlayer connection modeling supplemented by time domain analysis to predict the growth trend of common-mode noise caused by multiple layer hopping pair mode transitions
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How can differential pairs be routed across BGA dense areas to balance fan-out efficiency and electrical consistency?
options: A

The outer ring uses the surface layer, and the inner ring uses HDI blind hole to fan out the inner layer; The symmetry window is reserved for the differential pair of channels to avoid phase shift caused by unilateral corners, and the coupling equilibrium is simulated to verify it
B

The through-hole structure is used in the inner area of the BGA to export all signals, and then uniformly converted to the inner layer to increase the consistency and mechanical strength adaptability of the connection between the upper and lower layers of the BGA
C
The dual-layer fan-out strategy with multi-point ground reference channel can effectively alleviate the signal bottleneck in the BGA region and improve the ground continuity and impedance integrity
D

Forced alignment of the differential pair at a 45-degree angle through the BGA grid gap and added an asymmetric compensation line structure below the centerline of the BGA solder ball to reduce signal distortion
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why does a differential pair trace fail even if the length matches when crossing the power island on a non-contiguous reference plane?
options: A

The power island is not constant, and the return path break will introduce different propagation delays and common-mode noise. It is necessary to introduce cross-ground capacitors or ground via network bridging to ensure the electrical equivalence of the path
B

Due to the frequent voltage fluctuations at the power supply island, it is difficult for the differential signal to maintain a stable spectral response, and even if the trace length is matched, waveform distortion and mode conversion interference may occur
C
Even if the length is the same, the discontinuous plane causes impedance mismatch and current loop path shift, which can cause reflection and EMI problems, especially at high speeds
D
Traces across different reference regions induce different EMC behavior characteristics, resulting in signal spectrum expansion, and signal integrity must be verified using hybrid simulation
E
None of the above options are correct

question: In high-speed interfaces above 10Gbps, how to accurately compensate for insertion losses caused by PCB differential traces?
options: A

It is necessary to combine the modeling of the dielectric loss factor, copper foil roughness and trace length, pre-add forward equalization (FIR) and time-domain reflection compensation (DFE), and add the dummy segment to the PCB to calibrate the eye diagram opening
B

Weaken high-frequency attenuation by lowering the driver level and shortening the PCB path length, while connecting variable capacitors in series at the receiver to optimize the transmission characteristic curve and improve the channel bit error rate
C

The asymmetric wiring structure is used to improve the common-mode bandwidth, supplemented by an active common-mode filter to dynamically adjust the compensation response interval to match the changes in the dielectric behavior of the material at different temperatures
D

Compensate for losses by introducing breakpoints in each differential path and inserting regulating inductors, while automatically adjusting the channel power spectral distribution in combination with echo modeling
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to evaluate the delay drift caused by temperature gradients in differential pairs? How to mitigate in multi-hot zone routing?
options: A

Only the geometric changes caused by thermal expansion and contraction on the length of the metal wire should be paid attention to, and the wire traces should be appropriately widened to increase the heat capacity to buffer the wave speed fluctuation
B
Real-time thermal imaging can be used to monitor the temperature changes of key traces and adjust the drive rate in combination with the MCU to dynamically maintain latency consistency
C

Delay drift is mainly caused by the change of dielectric constant with temperature. The hot zone should be traced to the balance compensation section through temperature control simulation modeling, and the propagation equivalence should be ensured by the constant temperature zone
D

Simply avoid the differential pair from crossing the heat sink or heat source area, without additional simulation, and the end-matching resistor adjustment can compensate for the propagation delay error
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What are the consequences of reversing the routing order of differential pairs (i.e., P/N signal crossovers)? In what scenarios is crossover allowed?
options: A

It will cause the differential signal to be out of sync and the energy shift will occur in multiple frequency bands, causing serious crosstalk interference, and the trace structure must be readjusted and shielding measures must be added to control the interference source
B
It will cause impedance jump and signal phase reversal, resulting in recognition error at the receiver end and aggravating the reflected echo
C

It will lead to the imbalance of the differential structure to form an asymmetric path, so that the common mode noise accumulates and superimposes, and in severe cases, it is necessary to insert the common mode choke and rewire it to ensure data integrity and anti-interference ability
D

Reverse electrical coupling and phase skipping, resulting in inter-code interference and radiation mismatch, are only allowed in the scenario of differential path symmetry, immediate restoration of sequence after transfer, and electrical compensation
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What layout techniques can be used to reduce modal transitions in long-distance differential pair traces?
options: A

A composite compensation design strategy for low-frequency common-mode filtering and enhanced stability of high-frequency signals can be realized by staggering differential lines and introducing heterogeneous reference layers at multiple nodes
B

It is recommended to use asymmetric structure traces and adjust the trace height, combined with the double-formation reversal compensation design to solve the discontinuity problem and improve the integrity and compatibility of high-speed signals
C

Periodic impedance perturbation and multi-segment filtering network should be introduced into the differential channel to reduce the risk of common-mode interference and crosstalk caused by long-line coupling together with the conductor shield
D

Limit the change of trace length, reduce multiple layer skips, maintain symmetrical wiring and matching impedance, and add common-mode inductors or absorbing terminals if necessary to alleviate the common mode accumulation effect
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What signal integrity issues are most likely to be overlooked in the transition region leading to the external connector? How to design to avoid?
options: A

Capacitors are often not attached near external connectors, resulting in severe signal reflection at low frequencies, and the return loss problem can be improved by replacing high-bandwidth connectors and adding absorber layers
B

Due to the common mode crosstalk coupling phenomenon in the transition section, it is necessary to use a three-layer shielding shell and a multi-point grounding structure with a common mode filter for system-level anti-interference design
C

Impedance incoherence is often caused by the lack of symmetric optimization of the signal path, and the number of interference channels can be reduced by shortening the length of the leader line and adjusting the layout of the connector
D

Connector pad reflection and plugging and unplugging lead to impedance mutations and ground protection discontinuities are most often ignored, and simulation should be used to extract S parameters, optimize the connector transition geometry, and symmetrically arrange the ground of the shell
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Is it always beneficial to use fening (de-solder mask) on differential pair routing? Why?
options: A

Differential distribution line fening can help improve symmetry and reduce the influence of via interference, but it may cause the signal density of the routing area to increase and the local complexity of the layout may increase
B

Reducing the dielectric constant of the window opening can reduce the propagation delay, but at the same time increase EMI and surface loss, it is necessary to balance the frequency and length, and it is recommended to maintain the full solder mask or simulate the window opening width > 5GHz
C

It can improve the surface conduction efficiency and reduce AC loss, but it is necessary to add a lead passivation layer and auxiliary grounding to avoid the increase of differential channel reflection coefficient and impedance deterioration
D

It is recommended to widely use solder mask treatment to enhance wiring flexibility, especially suitable for high-speed chips and strong interference occasions, which can effectively improve electrical performance and system stability
E
None of the above options are correct

question: If the differential pair must be cross-routed with other differential pairs, how to design a coupling interference suppression?
options: A

The orthogonal cross wiring structure is adopted, and the intersection section is shielded to control the length of the intersection area less than the coupling length constant, and the symmetry of the intersection point is maintained to avoid deflection interference
B

An additional grounding network can be introduced below the intersection point, and the distance of the crossover section can be extended to reduce the interference of the common-mode signal, and the frequency consistency and anti-crosstalk ability of the entire plate structure can be enhanced
C

It is recommended to widen the impedance and increase the spacing of the intersection segments, and apply bypass capacitors at the intersection nodes to mitigate the symmetry breakdown of the differential path and the increase of dipole radiation
D

When crossing, the trace height step staggering method is used to guide the signal to avoid the high coupling path, and at the same time, the inter-board coupling is suppressed by embedded blind holes to optimize the overall signal propagation speed
E
None of the above options are correct

question: When a differential pair is connected to a SerDes chip at one end and a hot-swappable backplane connector at the other end, how can the reflected wave interference caused by multi-source impedance mismatch be mitigated in the cabling?
options: A

Absorbent material can be introduced into the middle of the wiring to fill and the microstrip groove can be used to suppress crosstalk synchronously, so as to balance the position of the signal reflection point and improve the echo stability characteristics of the whole board
B

It is recommended to adopt a stepped impedance design and add a series small inductor to each segment to improve the disturbance effect caused by discontinuity on the spectral response of the differential channel during the plugging and unplugging process
C

It is necessary to add an impedance matching structure at both ends, add a controllable impedance transition zone to the backplane interface segment, optimize the continuity of the wiring spacing and reference surface, and add a pre-grounded end to the front section of the plugging and unplugging interface and pull away the reflection node
D

The SerDes chip drive capability should be maximized and multiple bypass paths should be configured to buffer reflected interference, while the backplane signal layer should be switched to low-speed mode operation
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How do differential pairs ensure that crosstalk and insertion loss are minimized when traversing long backplane connection zones?
options: A

It is recommended to use variable width traces with staggered adapter channels, and use custom connector structures at the connection ports to prevent EMI interference and signal reflection affecting the back-end circuit
B

Differential pairs are embedded in the backplane with shielded channels with ground guards, and the wiring and ground vias are arranged in a staggered array, using low-loss media (e.g., Megtron) and simulating delay matching and proximal crosstalk
C

By alternating the differential line with the single-ended signal and inserting the dielectric adjustment layer, the asymmetric electromagnetic field structure can be used to guide the signal to avoid the coupling sensitive area to improve compatibility
D

The use of high-frequency and high-speed impedance bandwidth amplification technology and the synchronous drive of the receiver pre-added equalizer to realize the adaptive transmission optimization scheme of dynamic speed regulation and channel loss adaptation
E
None of the above options are correct

question: In a PCB system with multiple high-speed differential pairs, if one of them is a "discontinuous pair" route (bypassing the intermediate obstacle), how should the overall equivalent delay be maintained?
options: A

It is recommended to place all differential pairs on the same layer to avoid intersection, and use ground grooving to guide the synchronous compensation of non-continuous paths to improve the delay alignment accuracy of the whole channel
B

In the discontinuous channel, the goal of differential coupling compensation balance of length equivalence and offset suppression is achieved by increasing the number of power via and fine-tuning the layout structure of the reference plane
C

The crosstalk path can be adjusted synchronously by stepped interconnection, and the interpolation line alignment path length can be introduced in the offset area to control the frequency offset problem caused by obstacle avoidance
D

The bypass part should adopt an isometric compensation strategy, cooperate with the local symmetrical structure to restore the electrical center position, and introduce a compensation stub or internal dummy line alignment delay if necessary
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What failure modes can occur when differential pairs span high-speed power layer cutting regions even with impedance matching?
options: A

It can lead to the reverse conduction amplification of the driver and cause ground return drift, which will amplify the interference source and cause data instability at the receiver when the signal changes frequently between layers
B

It is easy to produce dynamic resonance coupling and uneven potential of the reference surface, and it is necessary to reduce the synchronous bit error caused by crosstalk and reflected waves by enhancing wiring symmetry
C

The interruption of the return path leads to the enhancement of the common mode current, which induces EMI, return loss, and even radiation exceeding the standard. The bridge should be encrypted at the cutting edge to keep the magnetic loop closed
D

The frequency interference and superposition of frequencies in the cutting area will affect the electromagnetic continuity and effective coupling strength adjustment ability of the upper and lower layers in the scenario of synchronous transmission of multiple signals
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why do differential pairs need special handling when the FPGA wiring layer crosses the PLL region? How should it be designed?
options: A

PLL is extremely sensitive to power supply noise, and the differential pair may induce harmonic coupling in this area, so the PLL power supply surface layer should be avoided, and a local shielding layer should be added if necessary and the loop area should be controlled
B

Differential signals can cause distributed distortion to the PLL reference clock, and a buffer must be introduced and a source filter layer must be added to prevent additional interference from the clock spectrum broadening
C

If the parasitic ground current path is easy to form in the PLL region without protective measures, the AC circulation can be blocked and the jitter tolerance can be improved by introducing a matching network
D

It is recommended to add an inductive loop isolation zone to the lower layer of the PLL core power supply area to block the high-frequency signal and prevent the relevant clock control circuit and phase offset from being accidentally triggered
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How do you control impedance continuity in the transition zone when using Semi-Flex PCBs for high-speed differential pair connections?
options: A

The full-area constant width design can be used to reduce the interface impedance fluctuation, and the combination of multi-point crimping can maintain the isopotential connection to adapt to the changes of signal patterns in different frequency bands
B

The dielectric constant of the semi-flexural region is different, and the impedance compensation wiring needs to be carried out at the rigid-flex interface, and the wiring width is adjusted according to the simulation, the shape of the transition zone is smooth to avoid sudden changes in the electric field, and the ground ring is added to stabilize the field distribution
C

The thickness of the ground plane should be increased in the rigid area and the isometric bypass traces should be laid synchronously to enhance the overall current uniformity and thermal diffusion efficiency, and at the same time reduce the structural mode conversion error
D

It is recommended to add tensile copper foil to the flexible layer and insert an intermittent dielectric layer to improve the consistency of signal propagation and reduce the high-frequency energy loss caused by impedance reflection boundary conditions
E
None of the above options are correct

question: If the thick copper power layer must be crossed on the differential pair lead path, how can the reverse induction effect be suppressed?
options: A

The stepped thinning power stacking structure should be given priority, and the traces should be widened at the via position to reduce the longitudinal interference path and improve the anti-crosstalk ability of the power layer
B

The up-and-down coupling ribbon line structure can be used to synchronously increase the differential distance, so that the center of gravity of the magnetic field is away from the power supply path to reduce the noise backcharge phenomenon caused by the switching layer
C

By adjusting the position of the thick copper layer reference plane and balancing the ripple current with the insertion of the bypass capacitor network, the interference of the differential pair of crossing area can be partially suppressed
D

It is necessary to design an equipotential via array to isolate the power layer from the ground plane, use a shielded ground via matrix to reduce the return common-mode noise, and maintain the consistency of capacitance across layers
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to do internal routing in the chip packaging layer to improve SI consistency when the Pad pin distribution is asymmetrical in the SOC chip?
options: A

The introduction of short stubs can be used to extend the propagation of path balance signals, and then the ground compensation loop can be used to improve synchronization and enhance the distribution consistency and phase response of the RDL layer
B

Direct trace along the shortest path and use metal chamfer to transition the impedance discontinuous region to reduce the multi-stage reflection offset interference caused by the encapsulated lead structure
C

It is necessary to straighten the line in the packaging RDL or substrate layer to ensure that the relative length is consistent, the corners are arranged symmetrically, and the symmetrical and homogeneous traces are preferred to avoid out-of-phase delays in the packaging internal differential line
D

It is recommended to pull the differential signals to different packaging regions before merging, and use a balanced extended line network for delay compensation to improve the synchronous tracking ability across layer segments
E
None of the above options are correct

question: When differential pairs are connected in series through multiple daughter cards (multiple intermediate connectors), how can the accumulation of overall echo mismatch be controlled in the cabling?
options: A

Local impedance leveling is required at the joint of each sub-card, the connector pad is added with an absorption structure or matching stub, and a step reference layer is used between the wiring to control the impedance reflection cascade
B

The coupling width and position of the differential line between each daughter card can be uniformly adjusted by using a centralized coupling path to avoid the amplification effect of mode conversion caused by the uneven reflection coefficient between segments
C

The impedance transition is weakened by inserting a double-layer symmetric trace with a common-mode ground through-hole filter structure, while fixing each connector structure in a symmetrical path layout
D

Recommending differential pairs using reversed-phase crossover in each segment and introducing a dynamic compensation delay mechanism to optimize the superposition effects of distributed crosstalk coupling in multilayer boards
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What are some of the imperceptible problems that arise when using differential pair auto-pairing mode in board-level auto-routing tools? How should it be manually checked?
options: A

Automatic pairing usually widens the differential spacing to adapt to the trace density, but at the same time causes impedance mismatch, it is recommended to adjust the differential trace endpoint uniformly and perform grounding optimization simulation
B

The tool may ignore details such as upper and lower layer symmetry, corner offset, and discontinuous reference layers, and check the symmetry of the skipping layer, the change of coupling spacing, the phase offset segment, and the reflection hotspot
C

If there is a repeat section in the differential path, the automatic wiring will forcibly fold back to adjust the path, and a differential equalization network and auxiliary drive structure must be introduced to offset the negative voltage excursion
D

The automatic wiring may not model the ground elastic effect, which can easily lead to the amplification of the delay difference of the multi-source signal, so a cross-compensation line should be added and the synchronization threshold condition should be evaluated
E
None of the above options are correct
